Double Integrals                                                                                                                    Special case: Ω is a rectangle
    I How can we find the area of a bounded subset Ω in the
      plane?                                                                                                                            I First we let Ω be a rectangle R = [a, b] × [c, d] and let
                                                                                                                                          f : R → R be a bounded function.
    I If f is a continuous and positive function defined on Ω,
                                                                                                                                        I Partition the interval [a, b] into m subintervals [xi−1 , xi ]
      then the equation z = f (x, y) represents a surface that
                                                                                                                                          by choosing a = x0 < x1 < x2 < · · · < xm = b.
      lies above Ω. What is the volume of the solid that is
      bounded below by Ω and above by the surface                                                                                       I Partition the interval [c, d] into n subintervals [yj−1 , yj ]
      z = f (x, y)?                                                                                                                       by choosing c = y0 < y1 < y2 < · · · < yn = d.
    I To answer these questions, we study the integral of                                                                               I These partitions induce a partition
      f : Ω → R, denoted by                                                                                                               P = { Rij : 1 ≤ i ≤ m, 1 ≤ j ≤ n } of the rectangle R

                              f (x, y) dA,

       where dA stands for “with respect to area”.

  double integral and repeated (or iterated) integral, are
  different; they only coincide when the function f is suﬀiciently
  well-behaved (such as being continuous).

Strategy to evaluate double integrals                                                                                         Example 8
    1. Draw the integration domain.                                                                                           Calculate   Z πZ π
                                                                                                                                                   sin y
    2. Trace a line parallel to the axis of the inner variable to                                                                                        dy dx.
                                                                                                                                           0   x     y
       determine the inner limits.
    3. Consider how much you can move the tracer line to
       determine the outer limits.
    4. Check that the outer limits must be constants and the
       inner limits can be constants or functions of the outer
       variable.
  Note
    I Sometimes it is easier to integrate in one order than the
      other.
    I Make sure to find the correct inner and outer limits when
      reversing the order. A sketch of Ω is useful and
      sometimes essential.                        .   .   .     . . . .     . . . .     . . . .       .   .       .   .   .                                           .   .   .     . . . .     . . . .     . . . .       .   .       .   .   .
